channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Christopher Nolan on the Anxiety of Filming ‘The Odyssey’ in IMAX
item.description: Filming entirely in IMAX meant new engineering and actor ingenuity. It was only halfway through the six-country shoot that he felt he could pull it off.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Erik Tanner for The New York Times
item.media:description: After reading “more translations than I care to remember,” Christopher Nolan realized “The Odyssey” was his ur-text: “It’s in everything I’ve done before.”

item.title: Fox, Bravo and BET Look to Take Microdramas Mainstream
item.description: Major networks like Fox, Bravo and BET are translating shows to the short-form video format that last year made $11 billion in global revenue.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams

item.title: ‘The Bear’: What to Remember Before the Final Season
item.description: The stressed-out staff at the Bear is back for one final dinner service and an uncertain fate. Here’s where Season 4 left off.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.media:description: Carmy dropped some very big news about his future role at the Bear near the end of Season 4.

item.title: Robert Townsend on ‘The Bear’ and Directing for Television
item.description: The filmmaker has lately turned to television, acting in FX’s “The Bear,” directing episodes of “The Chi” and mentoring Black actors and showrunners reared on his work.
item.dc:creator: Julian Kimble and Gioncarlo Valentine
item.media:credit: Gioncarlo Valentine for The New York Times

item.title: Is Canada About to Enter the Eurovision Song Contest?
item.description: As Canada’s government looks more toward Europe and less to the United States, its national broadcaster has become a full member of the group that organizes the glitzy singing event.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Ian Austen
item.media:description: The Eurovision Song Contest final in Vienna in May.

item.title: ‘Supergirl’ Review: This Glass Ceiling Is Made of Kryptonite
item.description: Milly Alcock is naturally appealing in this derivative, if altogether watchable, superhero movie.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: ‘The Invite’ Review: Who’s Afraid of a Last-Minute Dinner Party?
item.description: In this witty comedy, Seth Rogen and Olivia Wilde star as a millennial couple whose tense marriage gets a wild jolt from their neighbors.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: ‘Superman’ and ‘Heated Rivalry’ Alums to Broadway in ‘Three Days of Rain’
item.description: A winter revival of the Richard Greenberg drama will star David Corenswet, Yvonne Strahovski and François Arnaud.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: From left: Theo Wargo/Getty Images; Monica Schipper/Getty Images; Daniel Cole/Reuters
item.media:description: The revival will star David Corenswet, left, Yvonne Strahovski, center, and François Arnaud, right.

item.title: Some Museums in the UK and France Reduce Their Hours as Heat Wave Sweeps Europe
item.description: Institutions with air conditioning or stone buildings can market themselves as a respite from extreme temperatures. But some are reducing their hours.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.category: Palais de la Porte Doree (Paris, France)
item.media:description: Outside the Louvre Museum, which reduced its opening hours because of the heat, in Paris on Wednesday. Other institutions with cool spaces are offering free admission.

item.title: For Gloomy Vancouver Fans, the World Cup Is Glorious
item.description: The city has the N.H.L.’s worst team and an M.L.S. franchise that is threatening to leave. But it was able to celebrate after hosting Canada’s first victory at a men’s World Cup.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:description: Fans marched to the World Cup game between Canada and Switzerland in Vancouver on Wednesday.

item.title: How to Meet 1,500 Artists in 5 Days at Upstate Art Weekend
item.description: Upstate Art Weekend, starting Thursday, brings together New York artists and audiences, and offers everything from icebergs to Arte Povera to keep you cool.
item.dc:creator: Will Heinrich
item.media:credit: Lila Barth for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Magazzino Italian Art Center in Cold Spring, N.Y., is just one of many destinations during Upstate Art Weekend.

item.title: ‘The Loved Ones’ Review: So Much for That Peaceful Irish Retreat
item.description: Sharp performances from Maryann Plunkett and the other cast members lend weight and texture to Erica Murray’s play, even when the script becomes predictable.
item.dc:creator: Rhoda Feng
item.media:description: From left, Maryann Plunkett, Alana Raquel Bowers and Clare O’Malley in “The Loved Ones” at Irish Repertory Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: A New Dance Festival Moves Beyond the Same Old, Same Old
item.description: The Milly Rock, haunting flamenco and falling bodies: The Lincoln Center Contemporary Dance Festival delivers quality with nary a pointe shoe in sight.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts

item.title: ‘Jackass: Best and Last’ Review: Johnny Knoxville and Friends Reunite
item.description: In what purports to be the final installment of the MTV franchise, Johnny Knoxville and friends reunite to reminisce.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: ‘Strung’ Review: A Tease of a Thriller
item.description: Chloe Bailey, Lynn Whitfield and Lucien Laviscount have delivered more of a camp classic than a taut psychological film.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: Late Night Grades the Attractions at the Great American State Fair
item.description: The fair has everything, Jimmy Fallon said, including “a Trump approval rating roller coaster, which has the biggest drop in history.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix

item.title: ‘Love Island USA’ Kicks Out Another Contestant for Racial Slur
item.description: After Alannah Keyser made her debut on Sunday, viewers quickly found a social media video in which she uses a slur while singing.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:description: Alannah Keyser joined “Love Island USA” on Sunday.

item.title: ‘Little Brother’ Review: Just the Two of Us
item.description: John Cena and Eric André star in this crude, but funny, movie about a real estate agent who encounters a blast from the past.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: Jerry Moriarty, Painter Whose Brushstrokes Elevated Comics, Is Dead at 88
item.description: A self-described “paintoonist,” Mr. Moriarty created cartoons with spare dialogue that reminded his admirers of poetry or Samuel Beckett’s plays.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald

item.title: Stream These 5 Movies and TV Shows Before They Leave Netflix in July
item.description: Two acclaimed series and two films by the director George Roy Hill are among the notable titles leaving for U.S. subscribers next month.
item.dc:creator: Jason Bailey
item.media:credit: Night of the Living Dead LLC, via Image Ten/Photofest
item.media:description: Kyra Schon in “Night of the Living Dead.”

item.title: David Clayton-Thomas, Canadian Singer of Blood, Sweat & Tears, Dies at 84
item.description: He was also the key lyricist of the Grammy-winning, genre-blending band, whose hits included “Spinning Wheel,” “And When I Die” and “You’ve Made Me So Very Happy.”
item.dc:creator: Stefano Montali
item.media:description: David Clayton-Thomas in 1973. With his burly physique and snarling, bluesy tenor, he stood out in Blood, Sweat & Tears, an ensemble that was filled with jazz- and conservatory-trained musicians.

item.title: Newbery Medalist Kwame Alexander Starts an Imprint at Sourcebooks
item.description: Kwame Alexander is starting up an imprint at Sourcebooks, an innovative publisher that has found success in giving authors larger roles in the publishing process.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:description: Kwame Alexander will publish the next sequel to “The Crossover,” his Newbery Medal-winning novel in verse, under his own imprint at the fast-growing publishing house Sourcebooks.

item.title: ‘Romería’ Review: What Actually Happened to Her Parents?
item.description: In this semi-autobiographical drama, a young woman in Spain whose birth parents died of AIDS-related illnesses faces a complicated reunion with her extended family.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: ‘Lucky Strike’ Review: Fighting a Battle Behind Enemy Lines
item.description: This World War II movie is a nuts-and-bolts survival thriller that recalls a bygone era of Greatest Generation patriotism.
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Rapold

item.title: ‘Drunken Noodles’ Review: Saucy Encounters
item.description: Over two blurred summers, an intern at an art gallery experiences solitude and pleasure with the men he encounters.
item.dc:creator: Chris Azzopardi

item.title: ‘Couture’ Review: No Common Thread
item.description: Angelina Jolie plays a woman preparing for a Paris runway show in Alice Winocour’s fragmentary look at the rarefied world of high fashion.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: ‘Bouchra’ Review: All Too Human
item.description: In this 3-D animation, a New Yorker who’s also a coyote tries to come to terms with her mother while working on an autobiographical movie.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Best Sapphic Romance Books, According to Ashley Herring Blake
item.description: The author Ashley Herring Blake recommends swoony Sapphic novels that celebrate love between women across eras and genres.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Herring Blake
item.media:credit: The New York Times; Author photo by Craig Pope

item.title: 5 Photographs That Shine in National Geographic’s New Museum
item.description: Visual storytelling is at the core of the National Geographic Museum of Exploration, which opens on Friday in downtown Washington, D.C.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: The new complex has 100,000 square feet of immersive exhibits and attractions.

item.title: Jimmy Fallon Teases Trump’s Great American State Fair
item.description: “There’s even a dunk tank with JD Vance and a drunk tank with Kash Patel,” Fallon said.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: A Record Sotheby’s Auction Thrills London After a Brexit Hangover
item.description: British auction sales have declined by nearly half since 2015, but Wednesday’s results hint at a return to form for London as an international auction center.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Delight to Your Day
item.description: Naming the moon, Sometimes Island and laughing in real life.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: A Billionaire’s Basquiat Collection Goes Up at Pérez Art Museum Miami
item.description: Ken Griffin, the hedge fund manager, looks for artworks with a “wow” factor, never mind the number of zeros on the price tag. Ten of his Basquiats are on loan to the Pérez Art Museum Miami.
item.dc:creator: Brett Sokol and Ysa Pérez
item.category: Perez, Jorge M, Art Museum of Miami-Dade County
item.media:description: Art handlers with Basquiat’s “Pez Dispenser,” from 1984, at PAMM. The show represents the collector Ken Griffin’s coming out as a major player on the Miami art scene.

item.title: ‘Henry VI,’ ‘Camping’ and 6 More Shows to See Now
item.description: New York in June is a bonanza of Off and Off Off Broadway productions. Here’s our guide to shows we recommend, many in the city’s coziest spaces (and closing soon).
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: A Show for All Seasons: Henry Moore’s Art, Reborn in Open Air
item.description: At the Kew Royal Botanic Gardens in London, a massive outdoor exhibition reveals a lifelong dialogue in the sculptor’s work between nature and human creations.
item.dc:creator: Emily LaBarge

item.title: ‘The Moment,’ ‘Daddio’ and More Streaming Gems
item.description: A pair of unconventional takes to the life of a musician bookend this month’s under-the-radar recommendations for your subscription streaming services.
item.dc:creator: Jason Bailey
item.category: Swamp Dogg Gets His Pool Painted (Movie)

item.title: An Artist Creates Moments for Play, and Solidarity
item.description: From the Queens Museum to Times Square, the British artist Sonia Boyce shows art intimately connected to community.
item.dc:creator: Aruna D’Souza
item.media:credit: Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York/DACS, London; Photograph by Olivia Lifungula for The New York Times

item.title: ‘In the Hand of Dante’ Review: A Not So Divine Folly
item.description: Not even a double dose of Oscar Isaac can rescue this fanciful, oversauced tale of stolen art and spiritual questioning.
item.dc:creator: Jeannette Catsoulis

item.title: When the World Cup Came to SoFi Stadium, He Got a Backyard View
item.description: SoFi Stadium, the $5 billion event venue near Los Angeles, is on global display for the soccer tournament. But for some who live close by, it is just another headache.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Jamie Lee Taete for The New York Times

item.title: What’s Behind Broadway’s New Musical Drought?
item.description: Hollywood actors in starry plays, skittish investors and gate-keeping theater owners have all contributed to an unusually tough climate for song-and-dance shows.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.category: The Curious Case of Benjamin Button (Play)
Midnight in the Garden of Good and Evil (Play)

item.title: The Pursuit of Immortality in The Times
item.description: William McDonald, who recently retired as the obituaries editor after nearly two decades, shares how subjects are chosen to be remembered in the newspaper.
item.dc:creator: William McDonald

item.title: Josh Johnson Muses on the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool
item.description: “Hey, kids, remember you wanted to go to Disneyland? Instead, we’re going to go see the world’s largest kombucha!” Johnson said of the pool renovation as a tourist stop.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Clive Davis and Whitney Houston’s Successful and Tragic Story
item.description: The record industry titan guided Houston from a young star to worldwide phenomenon. But their story together was not without controversy.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:description: In Whitney Houston, Clive Davis recognized the potential of a powerful mezzo-soprano as an instrument to reshape modern pop music.

item.title: Lin-Manuel Miranda’s ‘Warriors’ Musical to Hit Broadway Next Spring
item.description: Miranda is co-writing the musical — his first since “Hamilton” — with Eisa Davis. It’s based on “The Warriors” film and novel.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Daniel Weiss for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lin-Manuel Miranda and Eisa Davis have jointly written the book, music and lyrics for their new musical, “Warriors.”

item.title: Lorcan O’Herlihy, Architect of Innovative Urban Housing, Dies at 66
item.description: Arguing that architecture is a “social act,” he built affordable homes on small lots in Los Angeles and elsewhere, with an eye toward smarter, more equitable design.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Michael Weschler for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lorcan O’Herlihy in 2000. “As global cities become denser and the need for housing greater, it is more critical than ever to design spaces that promote equity, human interaction and cultural evolution,” he said.

item.title: 7 Songs That Spun My Head Around
item.description: Hear a Rosalía tour highlight, forgotten new-wave from the 1970s and more.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:description: Rosalía’s dazzling Lux Tour hit Madison Square Garden last week.

item.title: Artist Pulls Work From London Museum After Clash Over Churchill’s Legacy
item.description: A historian and others said that a video installation had incorrectly blamed Winston Churchill for a famine in colonial India.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:credit: Jeremie Souteyrat for The New York Times
item.media:description: The National Portrait Gallery in London. A video installation called “Persistence” was taken down this week after a historian criticized a reference to Winston Churchill.

item.title: Manhattan Borough President Gives $50 Million to N.Y.C. Arts Groups
item.description: In allocating the borough’s discretionary budget entirely to cultural projects, Brad Hoylman-Sigal said he wanted to send a message to President Trump about the need to keep arts funding.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.category: LaGuardia, Fiorello H, High School of Music and Performing Arts
Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture

item.title: Can’t Stop Second-Screening? Netflix Is Counting on It.
item.description: A new game featuring the voices of Zoë Kravitz and Sadie Sink turns a user’s phone into a controller in an attempt to combine the first and second screen experience.
item.dc:creator: Megan Farokhmanesh

item.title: Up Late With Botticelli as the Uffizi Gets a Reset
item.description: The museum’s director, Simone Verde, is working long hours as he tries to remake the institution into a living encyclopedia.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo and Clara Vannucci
item.media:credit: Clara Vannucci for The New York Times

item.title: 2026 Jimmy Awards: With Bowen Yang as Host, Teen Winners Are Crowned
item.description: Students from Georgia and Arizona won the top prizes at the ceremony, which was hosted by Bowen Yang and celebrates excellence in high school musical theater.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: Mourning After the Michael Jackson Movie? It Might Be ‘Michosis.’
item.description: Some Michael Jackson fans are experiencing deep, lingering grief after watching the biopic — a potent reminder that he is gone, they say.
item.dc:creator: Farah Fleurima
item.media:description: Jaafar Jackson as Michael Jackson in the recent biopic “Michael,” which has set off a wave of renewed mourning over the pop star’s death.

item.title: ‘The Sound of Music’ and ‘A Few Good Men’ Are Coming to Broadway
item.description: Lincoln Center Theater, basking in the glow of its Tony-winning “Ragtime” run, plans revivals of two more well-known titles this season.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson

item.title: Photos Behind Kennedy Center Tarps Show No Sign of Trump’s Name
item.description: Images circulated by an activist group reveal bare marble where President Trump’s name once resided. The Kennedy Center previously told a federal judge it had been removed.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: In a sworn declaration this month, the Kennedy Center said it had removed the words “The Donald J. Trump and” from its facade.

item.title: A New ‘Odyssey’ Audiobook Puts the ‘A.I.’ in ‘Michael Caine’
item.description: The longtime Christopher Nolan collaborator isn’t in the director’s forthcoming Homeric adaptation. But a new audiobook sets Caine’s voice off on its own adventure.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:description: Michael Caine has licensed his voice to the A.I. company ElevenLabs.

item.title: Rare Books on Sex Have Spiced Things Up at a Library Franklin Founded
item.description: The Library Company of Philadelphia, created in 1731 by Benjamin Franklin, has received a gift of 1,500 volumes about sexuality dating back to the 17th century.
item.dc:creator: Ralph Blumenthal and Caroline Gutman
item.media:description: Charles E. Rosenberg has been giving rare books to the Library Company of Philadelphia since the 1960s. His most recent gift was a collection of some 1,500 texts about sexuality.

item.title: Charles Hinman, Who Brought New Dimensions to Painting, Dies at 93
item.description: Starting in the early 1960s, the New York artist blurred the line between painting and sculpture with his shaped, protruding canvases.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:description: Mr. Hinman in front of his works in 1967. Unlike the standard flat rectangular painting, some of his featured undulating curves; others, contours like cut gems.

item.title: Plays, Musicals and Theater Festivals Across the U.S. Worth Traveling For
item.description: Across the country, audiences will find an abundance of Shakespeare, exciting new plays, and musicals and regional repertories in bucolic settings.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes and Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.category: New York Stage and Film Co
Muny (Municipal Theatre Association of St Louis)
item.media:description: The opening moment of the play “As You Like It” at Hudson Valley Shakespeare in Garrison, N.Y.

item.title: Late Night Dunks on Trump for Hiring Greenwater Services
item.description: Jimmy Fallon poked fun at the name of the business under fire for turning the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool bright green: “Nailed it.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)
Last Week Tonight With John Oliver (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Clive Davis Knew to Let Patti Smith Have Free Rein
item.description: For Patti Smith, the best guidance was something that Davis rarely granted: free rein.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Ron Galella, Ltd./Ron Galella Collection, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Patti Smith and Clive Davis in 1993.

item.title: Carlos Santana, Patti Smith and Other Celebs Pay Tribute to Clive Davis
item.description: Davis “treated me with the same respect and kindness as a 22-year-old nobody as he did after all my success,” Springsteen said after the music executive’s death on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Leon Bennett/Getty Images for the Recording Academy
item.media:description: Clive Davis and Gladys Knight at a pre-Grammy Awards gala in 2024. “There will never be another Clive,” Knight said after Davis’s death.
